messages.content: Hey it's Aanya again. So I got my TSH done and it came back normal, 2.1 mIU/L. Really relieved about that honestly.

That's great news — 2.1 mIU/L is well within the normal range and actually quite reassuring given your family history. I've updated your record with this result and the date. This is a good baseline to compare against in future annual checks. How have things been going otherwise since we last spoke in March?
I've actually been doing better overall. Started taking my vitamin D supplement more consistently, just set a phone reminder. My energy levels feel slightly better I think, hard to tell though.

The phone reminder trick clearly worked — consistency is everything with supplements, and even a few weeks of regular intake can start moving the needle on vitamin D levels. The energy improvement might be subtle at first but it tends to compound over time. When's your next blood test? It would be worth rechecking your Vitamin D level in another 2–3 months to see if the supplementation is bringing you up to the 30+ ng/mL range.

I wanted to talk about my diet a bit. I've been trying to go mostly vegetarian for the past few weeks. Not fully, but like 80-90% of my meals are plant based now. I'm a bit worried about protein and B12 since I was already anemic.

Really valid concern — this is actually one of the most common nutritional pitfalls when transitioning to plant-based eating, and it's even more relevant for you specifically given your existing anemia. Your iron concern is real: plant-based iron (non-heme iron) is less bioavailable than animal-based iron. You'll need to be strategic about it. For protein, lentils, chickpeas, tofu, paneer if you still eat dairy, quinoa, and Greek yogurt are all strong options. For B12 — this one is critical and often overlooked. B12 is almost exclusively found in animal products, so on an 80-90% plant-based diet you should genuinely consider a B12 supplement. Long-term B12 deficiency can cause neurological issues and worsen anemia. Have you discussed this dietary shift with your doctor yet?

I introduced a lot of lentils and chickpeas into my diet. Also started eating more spinach for iron but I read somewhere that plant-based iron isn't absorbed as well? Is that true? Should I be worried?

You read correctly — and this is actually a really important nuance. Plant-based iron is non-heme iron, and its absorption rate is roughly 2–20% compared to 15–35% for heme iron from meat. Spinach is a bit of a double problem: despite being high in iron, it also contains oxalates which actually inhibit iron absorption. So it's not the best iron source despite its reputation. Better plant-based iron sources for you: lentils (excellent), fortified cereals, tofu, pumpkin seeds, and dried apricots. The good news is that pairing any plant iron source with vitamin C significantly boosts absorption — so a squeeze of lemon on your lentils or a small orange with your meal makes a real difference. Given your existing anemia, I'd keep taking your iron supplement alongside the dietary effort rather than relying on diet alone.

The headaches have gotten slightly better since I started drinking more water. I think I was just chronically dehydrated honestly. Still getting them maybe 2 times a week now instead of 3-4, so that's progress.

That's genuinely meaningful progress — going from 3–4 headaches per week down to 2 is a real improvement, and the fact that hydration alone achieved that strongly suggests dehydration was a major driver. I've updated your headache frequency from 3–4 times per week to approximately 2 times per week. Keep the hydration habit going — aim for 2–2.5 litres a day, more if you're exercising. If headaches continue to improve, it'll also help you reduce that ibuprofen reliance, which is good for your gut and avoids the rebound headache risk we talked about.

My anxiety has been a bit worse lately. Work has been really stressful, we have a big product launch coming up and I've been working late almost every day. I've been sleeping only around 5-6 hours on weekdays which I know is terrible.

It is — I won't sugarcoat it. 5–6 hours is genuinely insufficient for most adults, and sleep deprivation is one of the fastest ways to amplify anxiety and reduce your ability to cope with stress. What you're describing — a high-pressure deadline, late nights, poor sleep, worsening anxiety — is a very predictable feedback loop. Each element makes the others worse. A few things that might help even in the short term: try to protect one consistent wind-down hour before bed even during crunch time, no screens if possible. Even getting to 7 hours instead of 5.5 makes a significant difference. How long until the product launch?
I noticed I've been grinding my teeth at night too, my jaw hurts when I wake up sometimes. Never had this before. Could it be stress related?

Almost certainly yes — bruxism (teeth grinding) is very commonly triggered or worsened by stress and anxiety, and the fact that it appeared alongside your current high-stress period is a strong signal. It's worth mentioning to your dentist at your next visit because prolonged grinding can wear down enamel and cause jaw joint issues over time. In the meantime, being conscious of jaw tension during the day helps — notice if you're clenching when you're stressed at your desk. Some people also find magnesium glycinate before bed helps with muscle relaxation and sleep quality. I've noted this as a new symptom — jaw pain from suspected nocturnal teeth grinding, onset around May 2024 during a high-stress work period.

Oh and I tried a new restaurant last week and had some prawns. My lips felt a little tingly after but I assumed it was the spice since the dish was really spicy. It went away after like 20 minutes. Probably nothing right?

It's probably fine, but I want to be honest — I wouldn't completely dismiss it. Tingling lips after eating a specific food, even if it resolves quickly, can be an early sign of oral allergy syndrome or a mild shellfish sensitivity. The spice explanation is entirely plausible too, especially if the dish was genuinely very spicy. The key things to watch for: does it happen again if you eat prawns or other shellfish? Does the reaction seem to intensify at all? For now, I'd note it as an observation rather than a confirmed allergy — but if it happens again, please tell me immediately and see a doctor before eating shellfish again. I'm logging this as: possible mild reaction to prawns in May 2024, tingling lips, resolved in 20 minutes, cause uncertain.
